speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five of the interview with Marion Horn. I'm Rivka Wakslak. It's September 13, 1995. You were telling me how you came to the United States. Where did you come first?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in New York City, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by boat. We spent a few days in New York. And my brother was a political science major, and he had worked at the UN in Geneva. So my parents thought that Washington, DC, and the government of the United States needed him desperately. And they were very disappointed when they found out that they didn't. [LAUGHS] Uh. My brother had gotten married in Switzerland. And he married a Swiss girl. And it was quite an experience for her to, first of all, the transition of getting married, and at that point marry a poor Jewish boy who came to a new country and didn't have a job. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did not speak a word of English when I came here. And at the dismay of my parents, I refused to study English in Europe. Because I explained to my father that I probably will have a horrible accent. And whatever accent will be-- once I learn the language, and I learn very quickly-- will be mine and not one from a either German, or French, or British professor. By the way, speaking of languages, I mentioned that I spoke fluent Yugoslav. And it's a total mental block. I absolutely completely forgot the language from one day to the next. And it's just something that I have no explanation for whatsoever. And I-- I just-- you know, it was very interesting to me, because I had no control over this. And usually I like to feel that I have some control over-- and very interesting. In any event, when we came here, we went to Washington, DC. The people that sponsored us were the Cohen family-- Cohen corduroy and Cohen denim. Very, very wealthy people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were your relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were cousins of my father, second cousins. And as a matter of fact, one of the brothers was mayor of Greensboro, North Carolina. And two of the sisters [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] have a tremendous art-- were tremendous art collectors. One was a physician and she graduated-- she was one of the first women graduating from John Hopkins. And they endowed the Baltimore Museum of Fine Arts. And they have a traveling-- they're dead of course, but, you know, at this point. But there's a tremendous collection that was shown only a couple years ago. And we fell into it by chance in Boston and the Cohen collection. Quite interesting. They gave us $100 when we came here and said, it was nice knowing you, good luck. And so a week after I was in this country, I went to an employment agency. And my only experience that I had was while going to school and studying I was a pre-med student-- laboratory. It's only thing I could think of. Not that I had vast experience in the lab, but just everyday experience-- taking tests and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I opened up a page in the dictionary, took a book off the shelf, and showed the young lady 'laboratory.' So she sent me for an interview at a very large hospital in DC-- old Garfield Memorial Hospital. And I went for an interview. And the pathologist that interviewed me was sitting behind his desk. I didn't understand a word he said. He didn't understand a word I said.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard somebody speaking Spanish somewhere, and I followed where the Spanish was coming from. I pulled that lady by her sleeve [LAUGHS] into the office. And between her Spanish and my Italian, I made myself understood and I got the job. But I was very underpaid. And I was told that it was a probationary job and they were going to see how I was doing. And, you know, so I started working. Didn't understand anything. And within about two or three weeks, I was able to understand what people were laughing about and talking about and. Started on the bottom in the urinalysis department. You know, that's where everybody starts. And after six weeks, I went to the pathologist. I knocked on his door and I asked them for a raise in English. [LAUGHS] And I almost sent him flying through the floor. I got the raise. And then I asked him if I also could-- the night person was leaving-- could I have the night job as well. And he said, you're much too skinny and too young. I'm afraid you're going to be sick with tuberculosis. You-- you're much-- I'm afraid. I said, I need the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to have--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only one supporting your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to have the two jobs. So this was-- I was making for the daytime job $45 a week and for the nighttime job $10 a night. I was in the money. The rent was $80 a month. And I was able to pay all the bills. And we-- we made it. Unfortunately, my sister-in-law got very sick. She had a terrible pregnancy. She got pregnant right away. And she really was very, very ill. So she was in the hospital. And then gradually my brother came to New York. And my mother followed him. And then eventually my sister-in-law followed, once she felt a little bit better. And then my brother-- my dad and I, we dragged it out for as long as we could. Because we just liked being together. [LAUGHS] And I didn't see him very much. He used to come visit me in the hospital. Because I never even came home anymore. I worked almost around the clock. So whatever times I had, I slept in the hospital, and grabbed any room that I could find and could lock a door so that I didn't have to travel. And then eventually I came to New York. And my first job-- I tried several jobs, different institutions. And I-- I was very selective, which you can be when you're young. I didn't just take any job at that point because I was really quite experienced. And in that hospital in Washington we had a rotation. So you spent one week in chemistry, one week in blood bank, one week in hematology. It was terrific experience. And then I got the job for the International Ladies' Garment Workers Union in New York to set up their lab. And I set up the lab, hired 16 people, ran the place pretty well. Didn't tell them how old I was, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't think I was 20. I was about-- not quite 20 years, 19. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I-- we-- we moved to Washington Heights, my family. We got a sublet. Apartments were very difficult to come by. Was right after the war. And all of the GIs were coming home. So it was very difficult. And I used to walk by Columbia Presbyterian as though it was a pure Gothic cathedral. I looked at it in awe. And I said, this is where I want to be some day. And I got a job there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out months and months later, that some of the administrators wanted to know where did this girl come from and who is she. We heard she's Jewish. How did she get in here? But it was too late. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I needed help in the department. And I ran the pathology department and OB/GYN. And I needed help. And I hired a black girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was called on the carpet and told, who gave you permission? I said, I was told that I could hire a qualified individual, and this girl is a recent graduate from Hunter College.

speaker: interviewee
text: They only hired graduates, even then. You had to. If-- to work for the university, you had to be a college graduate. This was many, many years ago. You had to have a BA or BS. And I said, I wasn't told that I was being restricted by color or creed. I hired who I thought was best qualified. Nobody told me. But if this is the kind of institution I'm working for, then I will submit my letter of resignation by tomorrow. I wrote a letter of resignation that was denied, and the girl was hired.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet you your husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: '58? Wow. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: '64. I was married before.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Is there anything-- anything you'd like to tell-- tell us anything now that's it's 50 years after the war, after all these experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: After all the experience, there are a few things that are very much on my mind. One is that, number one, we're not living in a perfect world, which we all know. Uh, hatred is a wasted emotion. I think we have to try and utilize any experience as a learning experience and try and turn it around. And no matter how desolate and how negative things are at the time, maintain a sense of-- humor is very difficult to say. It's-- it's much more than that. Decency, ethics, your own standards of morals. And just because it's a dog eat dog world around us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] doesn't make it right to become the same way, because then everything is for naught. And I think the whole idea is to learn and to try and make this a better place, if we possibly can. Love the living things. Whether it's our children, the beasts around us-- animal world. But love, and reach out, and give back what you have. My father told me as a little child there were a lot of beggars in Yugoslavia. And he would always say to me, sweetheart, never pass by a beggar. Maybe they're phony, but nobody likes to be on their knees begging. Whatever you give will come back to you tenfold. Share whatever you have with others. And give. And I think that's-- it's worked for me. Because I have gotten back tenfold. I've been able to work. I love the work that I do. I'm a cytologist. I feel privileged. The fact that maybe when-- and I'm not trying to be presumptuous now, but maybe when things are said and done, maybe I've helped save a few lives. And by early detection of cancer and doing my work, and doing it properly, and doing it seriously, we can help. And I think that's the key to our whole existence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if we can apply this-- and not just talk about it, but apply it. And no matter how small a job-- and I've had people in the hospital who said, yeah, but I'm only the cleaning person here. And I say, do you realize how important your job is? You're keeping us safe from bacterias, from germs in our lab by cleaning the way you do. Your job is important. Everybody's job is important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think if we can just-- nobody should look down at any job that anybody does, but realize that we all-- if we all do our part, and we do it well, that's all that matters. And that's what I'm trying to instill in my grandchildren. No matter what you decide you're going to do, but whatever you do, do it honestly and do it well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much, Mrs. Horn, for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for coming to my home and for being with us. It's been a privilege.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are we looking at here, Marion?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're looking at my mother and father's wedding day, which was on May 18, 1919, when my mother was 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, a very dear friend of my mother's who she grew up with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was almost-- well, we called her aunt. And she is a lady that visited us very, very regularly. She came from Romania and lived in Berlin and in Munich, and eventually lived in Houston, Texas, and managed to somehow escape the horrors of the war. Her husband was a chemical engineer and worked for Shell Oil Company. And she had a lot of photographs of us when we were children and of my mother. And they were girlhood friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I came fortunately by the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my father. Um, I assume that his age was-- at that time was in his late 50s. And my mother in her probably early 40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the apartment building where my parents lived in Munich, and where I was born, the top floor all around the corner. And that was on 9 Elisabethstrasse in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my maternal grandmother, who was a-- her husband died for-- World War I. Picture of myself and my brother. And my grandmother remained in Germany, because she thought she was a German war widow. And even at my father's continuous, uh, insistence refused to leave, and died in Theresienstedt ultimately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our house in Switzerland, which had a name. It was named Kia Ora-- Villa Kia Ora-- which means 'you're always welcome.'

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before the war. And it was on Monte Bre in the little village of Suvigliana.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother in my father's study and library. And it was taken probably in the mid '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Lugano.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lugano. What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we see this is a picture of my brother and I. Uh, there was a 5 and 1/2-- almost 6 year-- age difference. I was very, very close with my brother. An even later on in life when we were adults, we spoke with each other every day. The picture on the right is-- I'm in the basket. Lugano, being a wine country, and this was used for harvesting the grapes. And I just love the picture, because it shows our relationship very closely. And it's just nice. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's picture of my mother, and my brother, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I would say probably around 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lugano?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my handsome brother. And this picture was taken at my daughter's wedding. And it's the last picture that I have of him, because he was killed in a automobile accident shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950-- in 1979. And I'm glad I have this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an engraving that was done by Benno Elkan in 1924 of my father. And the artist, uh, has-- is renowned for his menorahs in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just a small sampling of the fine linen work that my father was involved in. And all of the things that you see here, all hand embroidered. The lace is all handmade. This was the work that was done in Yugoslavia. And there are only maybe a handful of stores in the-- in the world that carry this type of material. The little handkerchief that you see there with the name 'Dodo' embroidered was my nickname. And the reason it was Dodo is because I couldn't say Marion when I was a little girl. So I called my self doh-doh-doh-doh-doh, and Dodo stayed and remained that way for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, what do we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we first arrived in the United States it was very difficult to get any permanent position for a woman who really never had a job in her life. But she was very handy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother. And she was a very handy lady. And she made these gloves as samples for Sax Fifth Avenue and [INAUDIBLE]. And then from her samples they were reordered. And at that time, these were rather costly gloves, much more than the OJ Simpson's. Uh, this is a picture of my children. On the left is, uh, my son-in-law and on the right is my daughter, who unfortunately couldn't be with us right now because she came home from work indisposed. And her-- this is Richard and Lilo. And on my right is the love of my life. That's Marty, my husband of 32 years. Behind him is Erica-- my sweetie-- Richard, and Evan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to say that I was looking forward to this because my wife wanted to expose her feelings about everything that she went through. I think she survived it beautifully and became a very well-rounded person, despite travails in her very early days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
